title: Lesson 246
transcript: We have defined what the word cryptography actually means. It means taking something and turning it into a secret. Now some of the most valuable and strongest algorithms that we use today such as the ones you've heard of like AES or triple DES or RSA they all do something very similar and that is they provide substitution and transposition services so in this section we're going to define what that actually means but if you think about the words substitution means that we take certain things bits typically and substitute them for other bits or take letters and substitute them for other letters. Transposition means we keep the same letters or bits but we move them around in such a way so that ultimately we turn something into a secret. What we need to remember is fundamentally the strongest algorithms that are used today that are embedded into all kinds of products and technologies and protocols to provide encryption services do exactly this. Substitutions and transpositions in order to take plain text and turn it into ciphertext. So let's look at this. First of all, substitution means exactly that. We take something and substitute it for something else. Transposition means we don't substitute, we keep what it is, but we move things around so that ultimately we provide again a way to provide secret services we take something and turn it into a secret here's a perfect example of what substitution looks like so if we have the word gubbins that we want to turn into a secret There's different ways that we can do it, but the idea of substitution is we're going to take each of the letters and substitute them for something else. If you watch the animation here, what happens is we can shift the alphabet by three characters. so that the A is represented by a D, a B is represented by an E, the C is represented by an F. So if we have the word gubbins, that we have to turn into a secret. Based on this substitution cipher that we've created here, the G would become a J. the U would become an X and so on as you see here because what we've done is we've shifted the alphabet by three characters which allows us to substitute each of the letters of the plain text into ciphertext. In other words, we've turned something into a secret. And before I move on to the next slide, here's the one thing that we need to remember. Simple substitution and transposition ciphers are referred to as mono-alphabetic ciphers. In other words, we only use one alphabet. And so simple substitution ciphers, just like the one that we've created here, do not hide patterns. And that's why we've chosen the word gubbins as an example. There is a pattern that exists in the plaintext. And obviously it's the two B's that the word gubbins has. And so even though we've used a simple substitution cipher, the two B's will produce the same pattern. patterns in the ciphertext or in the secret. And so what you need to remember is that simple substitution ciphers do not get rid of patterns. And the worst thing that you can have in cryptography is patterns. Patterns is what kills cryptography. And so simple or mono-alphabetic ciphers do not hide patterns because the ciphertext that you've created still has patterns which allows attackers to figure it out eventually. what the key is now here's what transposition is and we're going to use the exact same word as an example but transposition means we don't substitute each of the letters we're going to move them around in such a way so that again we create a secret that hopefully isn't too obvious for attackers to figure out So we move the G to that position and then we move the U to a different position and so on. So if we keep doing this with the entire word gubbins, we might come up with something like bin bugs for example but again we've moved things around so that someone looking at the resulting ciphertext doesn't immediately know that the real word that we've hidden is the word gubbins but here's the point that we just made. Even though we're using transposition here as opposed to substitution it does not get rid of patterns. Patterns are still visible. So when we had for example the two bees we still have the two B's in the resulting ciphertext. Does not hide patterns and as we said patterns are not good in cryptography. You want to avoid patterns. Here's another way that you know a lot of ciphers and algorithms out there take plain text and turn it into ciphertext. In this case, we're looking at something called the rail fence. And what you do here is you create different rails that are gonna come up with or build your fence, for example. So let's say we've got three rails here. and we write our secret message, which in this case is security through obscurity. But the way that we write it is that zigzag that you see up on this slide here. And then to come up with our ciphertext, we're gonna take each of the letters that appear in the first rail, and then we're going to put the next letters after that from the second rail and the third rail. So if you look at this, what it creates ultimately as ciphertext is what you see on your screen. Now somebody looking at that is obviously going to think that this is very secure. and it's not very easy to interpret, but the bottom line is we've used a different way to do transposition. We've still kept the same letters, and guess what? This does not hide patterns either. because the same letters and whatever patterns appeared in the plaintext will still be somewhere in that resulting ciphertext. So even though it looks like a really complex way to turn something into a secret, it does not hide patterns. And as we've said many times, patterns need to be avoided. in cryptography so that we don't have any weaknesses that result in how we implement cryptography solutions. Here's another way to look at how transposition works and this is a typical method that a lot of algorithms will use and that is a table. And what we do is we write our message into the table, as you can see here, security through obscurity, but then how you use the table to come up with your ciphertext is really what becomes the key in how you produce the resulting ciphertext. So we could go down the columns, which would mean S, T, U, C, and so on. And if we keep going, our resulting ciphertext is what you see here up on the screen. or we can be more creative. We can start in a corner of that table somewhere and go diagonally to come up with our ciphertext. But regardless, The way that we use the table becomes the key that has to be passed to the receiver at the other end to be able to read that message. But again, these are simple substitution and transposition ciphers that ultimately do not hide patterns the patterns will still appear no matter how we've used this table the same patterns will always still appear. Now the last thing that we're going to talk about here in this section is that obviously cryptography algorithms will use each letter one at a time But that's how we've shown you what substitution and transposition actually looks like. Real cryptography does not use letters in order to turn something into a secret. It will always use bits. So if we were really to encrypt the word Gubbins, any real cryptography algorithm would actually take those letters, turn them into the bits, the zeros and the ones that represent those letters and then substitute and transpose the bits of information. So if you think about how we've got data that is represented by bits, zeros and ones, how you work with those bits is the difference between synchronous and asynchronous. Synchronous typically means we work with each bit synchronized through clock signals, for example. and asynchronous simply means that we use possibly chunks of bits and the input is dictated by the user somehow. So that's the difference between synchronous and asynchronous. Synchronous is dictated by clock signals, asynchronous is dictated by some input that is required by some other element or entity.
